# Questions in Livestream 156

Live Stream #156: The Pursuasion Dictionary

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:01</a> | Who put the "Musa" in Wadi Musa? | He says the Arabic name was applied by later Muslims, not by Israelites. | He explains that `Musa` is Arabic for Moses and that a wadi is a seasonal river or flash-flood channel. In his answer, many sites in the region were renamed after biblical figures when Muslims took over, especially when earlier names were pagan or no longer understood. He therefore treats Wadi Musa as a later Muslim naming, not evidence of an Israelite-era association with Moses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:20</a> | Is Genesis 37 evidence for a clan reading of the patriarch narratives? | He says the already distinct Ishmaelite identity fits a broader clan-development reading better than a very compressed literal sequence. | He reads Genesis 37:25-28 as significant because by Joseph's time the Ishmaelites already appear as a recognizable caravan people with their own clan or tribal identity. In his view, three biological generations from Abraham are too few to produce multiple distinct clans, since clan structures usually require more generational depth. That makes the passage fit better with a clan reading that allows more intermediaries between patriarchal names, even if the text does not state that explicitly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=822" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:42</a> | What name did Egyptians use for the Mitanni kingdom? | He says the Egyptian form is the Naharin or Naharina designation. | He answers briefly that Egyptians called Mitanni by a form like Naharin or Naharina. He adds that a related term appears in biblical usage as well, so the designation is not just an Egyptian label but part of the wider ancient terminology for that northern Mesopotamian region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=858" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:18</a> | Are there words in the Bible's original languages whose translation is still contested? | He says yes and recommends Paul Lawrence's book on uncertain biblical words as a useful guide. | He answers by pointing to Paul Lawrence's book, which he says collects disputed or uncertain biblical vocabulary such as food terms, machines, musical instruments, birds, animals, plants, precious stones, clean and unclean terminology, and idioms. He uses the Job 6:6 example of whether a term means egg white or turnip to show that some translations remain debated. His answer presents the book as a resource for seeing how many original-language words are still contested. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=1137" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:57</a> | Which Egyptian god was most disliked, why were there no pagan monasteries, and how were stolen tomb goods resold? | He names the Aten as the most disliked, says monasteries are a Christian-era development, and explains that tomb goods were often dismantled or reworked for resale. | He identifies the Aten as probably the most disliked Egyptian deity because Akhenaten's reign made a previously minor god deeply unpopular afterward. For monasteries, he says Egyptians did develop monasticism in the Christian era through the desert fathers, but Egyptian religion itself was a ritual system centered on sacred space and hierarchy rather than personal devotional monastic life. For stolen tomb goods, he describes dismantling objects for gold foil or bronze cladding, disfiguring items to hide ownership, and sometimes repurposing whole coffins or private-tomb goods, with the method depending on the source and period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=1412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:32</a> | Where can someone get a rough timeline for Judges and Joshua, and where does Ruth fit? | He places Ruth in the judges period, roughly four generations before David, and points to standard timeline tools. | He says Ruth belongs somewhere in the period of the judges because she stands about four generations before David. He does not claim a precise placement, suggesting probably sometime in the middle of that era, and recommends Kenneth Kitchen's chronology work because Judges and Joshua are not simply chronological but arranged didactically and with overlapping timelines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=1481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:41</a> | What are his thoughts on the Hindu mathematician who attributed mathematical discoveries to visions from his gods? | He says the mathematician was a genius, and the reported visions could be viewed psychologically or spiritually. | He says he has heard of the mathematician and describes him as a devout Hindu from a traditional priestly family with an extraordinary aptitude for mathematics. He thinks a skeptical reading could explain the discoveries as subconscious genius, while a non-skeptical Christian reading could allow that demonic entities might communicate knowledge if they chose. He stresses that the event cannot be recreated or finally tested from the transcript alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=1623" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:03</a> | Would attributing Hindu mathematical visions to demonic power lessen Christianity's credibility? | He says no; it fits a Christian worldview in which demons can have real but subordinate power. | He argues that attributing such visions to demonic power would not weaken Christianity because Christianity already allows for angels, demons, and other spiritual powers below the one supreme God. In his framing, Hindu gods are phenomenologically closer to Christian demons than to the God of the Bible, so the event would fit monarchial monotheism rather than competing with it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=1735" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:55</a> | Do the gospel instructions about taking a staff contradict one another? | He says the commands can reflect wording or circumstance differences, while the shared point is dependence on God rather than self-provisioning. | He distinguishes the wording in Mark, Luke, and Matthew: Mark can be read as allowing a staff already in hand, Luke says not to bring one, and Matthew says not to procure one. He argues all three are aimed at the same local-mission principle, namely that the apostles should not stock up but trust God to provide. He also leaves room for Jesus giving different practical instructions at different times or in different towns, so the staff question should not override the shared point of the passages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=1958" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:38</a> | Was Stephen wrong to say seventy-five people went down to Egypt? | He says this is better treated as a textual-tradition issue tied to Septuagintal counting than as a simple mistake. | He frames the seventy versus seventy-five count as a text-critical issue. The Masoretic tradition has seventy, while the Septuagint and some Dead Sea Scrolls evidence preserve seventy-five; one explanation is that additional descendants of Joseph beyond Manasseh and Ephraim are included. He therefore sees Stephen as using the common Septuagintal reading of his day rather than making a straightforward historical error. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=2103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:03</a> | Why are so many Egyptian gods pictured as humans with animal heads? | He says Egyptian gods were understood as personal agents with humanlike traits, while animal features signaled particular divine associations. | He says Egyptians treated gods as personal agents with consciousness, decisions, and other humanlike traits, which explains the human body in many depictions. The animal element signaled an added association: a cow head could evoke fertility, Anubis's jackal form connected to jackals around tombs and the afterlife, and Thoth could be shown as a baboon because of baboons' morning calls. He also notes that Egyptian gods could be shown as full animals, so the mixed form was one option within a broader symbolic system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=2324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:44</a> | Have Muslim Egyptologists tried to explain the Quran's claim that Pharaoh drowned in the sea? | He says Muslim Egyptologists generally avoid the issue; non-Egyptologist apologists sometimes propose Merneptah, but the evidence works against that. | He says Muslim Egyptologists generally stay away from the question, partly because most Egyptologists are not focused on biblical or Quranic apologetics and partly because the evidence makes the issue difficult. He mentions Maurice Bucaille as a well-known non-Egyptologist apologist who proposed Merneptah, but rejects that fit because the Merneptah Stele already places Israel in Canaan in Merneptah's fifth regnal year, while Merneptah lived several more years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=2502" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:42</a> | What is his favorite song, and can he dance? | He says he has no single favorite song, has eclectic musical tastes, and can dance. | He says he can dance and specifically mentions having done Regency dancing, including around the time he and his producer first met at a science-fiction convention. On music, he says he does not have one favorite song because his tastes are very eclectic, shaped in part by working in a college music department and being exposed to genres beyond radio, including alternative music, soundtracks, electronica, classic rock, and even some Vocaloid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=2677" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:37</a> | What is Vocaloid? | He describes it as synthesized singing made from human voice libraries and sound fonts. | He explains Vocaloid as a digital singing voice built from a human vocal library, similar in concept to a sound font for an instrument. He says it works especially well for staccato languages such as Japanese, where syllable timing is relatively regular, and less well for English. His answer is brief and definitional rather than a broader music discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=2759" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:59</a> | What is his favorite Bible verse? | He chooses 1 Peter 2:24 because he says it compresses the gospel and atonement into a theologically rich verse. | He chooses 1 Peter 2:24, saying that although he used to be especially drawn to John 3:16, he now gravitates toward this verse because it contains the whole gospel in miniature. He emphasizes that Christ bore plural sins in his body on the cross, that atonement was made in Christ's flesh, that believers are expected to die to sin and live to righteousness, and that the healing language also evokes Isaiah 53. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=2944" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:04</a> | What are his thoughts on Bishop Mari Emmanuel's stabbing incident? | He says the attack was a terrible crime, while noting Emmanuel is a controversial preacher who directly challenges false religion. | He calls the stabbing a terrible and dastardly crime, emphasizing that Emmanuel was attacked while giving a sermon. He also describes Emmanuel as a controversial figure who directly challenges false religions and preaches the gospel, so he says he is not surprised that someone like him would be targeted, while still treating the attack itself as criminal and wrong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=3251" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:11</a> | What are his thoughts on theosis? | He says there is a grain of truth if theosis means glorification in Christ, but it can be taken too far. | He defines theosis as an Eastern Orthodox doctrine in which those in Christ are glorified and receive some divine attributes after death. He says everlasting life is one sense in which a divine attribute is extended to humans, but rejects versions that imply humans become omnipresent, omniscient, or otherwise deity-like. He emphasizes that resurrected humans remain embodied creatures rather than all-powerful divine beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=3382" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:22</a> | Is God omnipresent? | He argues God is not omnipresent in essence, distinguishing God's essence from God's energy and connecting that to sacred-space theology. | He uses the Eastern Orthodox essence-energy distinction: God's energy can be found anywhere, but God's essence is not omnipresent. He says God remains omniscient and all-powerful, but if God were essentially present everywhere, it would collapse into pantheism and make God physically present at every evil event. He connects this to sacred-space theology by saying God retreats from evil rather than being essentially present in it, and he claims "all-present" creates theological problems that other divine attributes do not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=3583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:43</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have gymnasiums? | He says not until the Greek period, though Egyptians did exercise and train outdoors for things like wrestling and martial arts. | He says gymnasiums as such do not appear in Egypt until the Greek period after Alexander. Ancient Egyptians did have places and practices for exercise, wrestling, and martial arts, but he describes those as outdoor activities rather than institutional gymnasium culture. He adds that Egypt's heat would have made enclosed gymnasium-style exercise unattractive for much of the time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=3652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:52</a> | What is his favorite comedian and type of comedy? | He prefers verbal comedy and names Aristophanes as a favorite. | He says he likes verbal comedy and mentions the British series `Yes Minister` as an example of the kind of word-based humor he enjoys. When pressed for a favorite comedian, he names Aristophanes, calling the plays hysterically funny. He also says he does not have much time to watch comedy now. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=3772" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:52</a> | How does Psalm 136 not prove Pharaoh's death in the Bible? | He says the key verb is a traditional rendering, and the text does not require reading Pharaoh himself as drowned. | He argues that Psalm 136:15 uses a traditional English rendering like "overthrew," while the Hebrew verb means more like shaking or brushing off. On that basis, he says the psalm says God shook off Pharaoh and his army into the Sea of Reeds, not that Pharaoh himself necessarily died there. He distinguishes the chariots, which other texts say drowned, from Pharaoh and the infantry, and he says Pharaoh may have remained at the palace or stopped elsewhere, preserving uncertainty about the exact logistics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=4153" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:13</a> | Is yoga demonic? | He distinguishes Western stretching from religious yoga, saying the closer it is to Hindu spiritual practice, the more spiritually dangerous it becomes. | He says the answer depends on what is meant by yoga. Western yoga that has been reduced to stretching is, in his view, unlikely to cause much trouble, but yoga practiced as an Indian religious discipline uses bodily practices for meditation and drawing closer to Hindu gods. He treats that religious form as spiritually dangerous, mentions spirit-channeling and Kundalini language, and contrasts it with forms of tai chi that he says are not normally used to summon or channel spirits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=4438" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:58</a> | How old are Western yoga and Indian religious yoga? | He says Western yoga is recent, while the Indian religious form is roughly 3,000 years old. | He distinguishes the modern Western stretching form from yoga as practiced in India. In his answer, Western yoga is only about 150 to 200 years old, while the Eastern or Indian religious practice goes back roughly 3,000 years. He gives the answer as a rough age estimate rather than a detailed chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=4467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:27</a> | What happened to bodies left on ancient Near Eastern battlefields? | He says bodies were usually looted, gathered into pits, and buried so fields could return to agricultural use. | He says battles usually took place in open agricultural fields, so the land had to be cleaned up afterward for farming. In his reconstruction, the bodies were stripped of weapons and armor, the goods could be resold by the farmer or villagers who had to do the cleanup, and the corpses were buried in pits. He links this to the seasonal timing of battles, which avoided the worst heat, heavy rains, and fully grown crops. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=4603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:43</a> | What is baptism for the dead in 1 Corinthians 15:29? | He identifies it as washing the dead before burial, which Paul uses rhetorically rather than endorsing. | He says the practice in Corinth involved washing a dead body on a slab before wrapping and burial, a custom that predated Christianity and had analogues such as washing before mummification in Egypt. Some Christians connected that death-washing with baptism of the living, but he stresses that Paul is not endorsing baptism for the dead. In his reading, Paul is asking why people would bother with such a practice if they denied resurrection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=4811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:11</a> | What does he think of Maccabees passages about prayers for the dead? | He treats them as a remnant of ancient Israelite cult-of-the-dead practice that Judaism later left behind. | He agrees that prayers for the dead in Maccabees look like a remnant of ancient Israel's cult of the dead that Judaism eventually stopped. He says such prayers do not help the dead person, though they may help the living process grief after losing someone. His answer therefore separates pastoral value for mourners from any claimed effect on the dead. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=4883" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:23</a> | Do Mormons literally baptize the dead, and how do proxy baptisms get applied to historical figures? | He says yes, and connects the practice to Mormon genealogy databases and proxy baptisms. | He explains that Mormons use genealogical records so living Mormons can be baptized by proxy for deceased people. He says this is why they maintain large ancestry databases and why controversial cases such as proxy baptisms for Nazi leaders have occurred. He also says names can enter the system through family, public, or genealogical records, so the practice can affect people who did not personally submit data. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=5445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:45</a> | What is the supplementary hypothesis regarding Moses' memoirs? | He says the Pentateuch began with base documents, including Mosaic material, that were later edited and supplemented. | He describes the supplementary hypothesis as the idea that the Pentateuch began as base documents that were gradually supplemented over time. In his conservative version, Moses prepared papers that were given to Joshua, and Joshua or later editors collated them, added bridging phrases such as "and Moses said to the people," and updated grammar, spelling, or explanatory glosses. He contrasts this with the documentary hypothesis by saying supplementary redaction keeps core documents intact instead of freely rearranging them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=5645" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:05</a> | Why was Menkaure's tomb more interesting? | He says Menkaure's pyramid complex used different materials and drew unusual later private devotion. | He says Menkaure built the smallest of the three Giza pyramids, but its materials and construction differ from the others. He especially emphasizes the funerary or memorial temple, where people returned for generations to give personal private devotion. In his view, Menkaure appears to have been well liked compared with Khufu and Khafre, and the offerings outside the temple are unusually interesting because ordinary people, not priests, seem to have made them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=5788" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:28</a> | Were assertive female romantic advances period-accurate in ancient Egypt? | He says yes; women in ancient Egypt were not culturally expected to be passive. | He says Egyptian women could be forward in romantic or sexual relationships and were not treated as merely passive partners. He says women could initiate courtship or marriage proposals, and that the more restrictive role of women came later with Islam rather than representing earlier Egyptian culture. He also notes that the openness had its own social problems, including adultery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=5979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:39</a> | Was Joseph refusing Potiphar's wife's advances period-accurate? | He says yes, especially where the woman was already married or socially dangerous to accept. | He says men could refuse women's advances in ancient Egypt, and that refusing a married woman was especially understandable. He points to the Tale of Two Brothers as an Egyptian parallel where a man refuses the aggressive advances of his brother's wife. His answer therefore treats both Potiphar's wife's forwardness and Joseph's refusal as culturally plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=6088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:28</a> | Does the Greek in Matthew 28 imply one name or three? | He says the singular "name" does not justify modalism; the text names Father, Son, and Holy Spirit without identifying them as one person. | He notes that "name" is singular in the Greek of Matthew 28, but says modalists and Oneness Pentecostals cannot use that to prove Jesus is the Father, Son, and Spirit. The text names the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, and he says reading them as one person requires importing an idea not stated by the passage. He argues unclear phrasing should be read by clearer texts such as Jesus' baptism, where all three appear, and summarizes the phrase as baptism in the name of the one God, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=6432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:12</a> | Why is Elijah so esteemed, and when was he replaced? | He says Elijah was esteemed because he did not die and was expected to herald God's kingdom, but Jesus supersedes him in Christian focus. | He explains that Elijah, like Enoch, avoided ordinary death and was understood in Second Temple thought as having gone directly to heaven. Malachi's expectation that Elijah would return before the kingdom made him especially important, particularly under foreign domination when people hoped for deliverance. In Judaism, he remains prominent in Passover tradition, while in Christianity John the Baptist functions as an Elijah type and Jesus becomes the central figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=6819" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:39</a> | What is the difference between being drunk and being a drunkard? | He says the difference is habitual addiction or destructive dependence, not merely becoming inebriated at a feast. | He uses the wedding at Cana and Jewish feast practice to argue that people at feasts could become intoxicated without being classed as drunkards. In his answer, a drunkard is someone habitually given to wine in a way that damages life, work, family, or health. He treats biblical warnings against drunkenness as warnings about habitual excess and addiction rather than every instance of festival inebriation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=7104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:24</a> | What kind of wine did Christ make? | He guesses red wine, roughly 10-12 percent alcohol, and uses the answer to distinguish being inebriated from being a drunkard. | He guesses that the wine Christ made was red wine and probably around 10-12 percent ABV, adding that Christ did not make inferior wine. He then distinguishes intoxication at a feast from being a drunkard, saying Jewish festivals and Passover meals expected wine drinking and that guests could become inebriated without that being the same as addiction or habitual drunkenness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=7461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:21</a> | What does `pesel` mean in Exodus 20:4? | He says it means a carved divine image or idol, usually made from wood or stone. | He reads Exodus 20:4 and says `pesel` means an idol or divine image, normally a man-made carved object from materials like wood or stone. He does not develop a broader argument from the word, stressing that its normal context is a constructed idol rather than every possible image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=7698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:18</a> | Is the cup of undiluted wine in Revelation a reference to a Passover cup? | He says it likely refers to the cup of plagues. | He connects Revelation's cup of undiluted wine to the Passover cup associated with the plagues, which he identifies as likely the second cup. He distinguishes it from the initiation cup, the redemption cup, and Elijah's cup, and says Revelation is drawing on the plague-cup imagery rather than an ordinary drinking-cup reference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=7789" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:49</a> | Are the Sea Peoples mentioned in the Bible? | He says the Bible uses "Philistines" as a catch-all label for the Sea Peoples, not only for the Peleset. | He says the Bible calls the Sea Peoples "Philistines," but uses that as a broad catch-all category. In his explanation, the Peleset are one tribe among the Sea Peoples and the source of the Philistine name, while other groups, including Amalek in his account, have distinct identities but still belong to that wider Sea Peoples context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=7856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:56</a> | Why might a popcorn-review comment not show up on YouTube? | He says YouTube or channel filters may delete links or language, and YouTube can also glitch. | He gives three likely explanations: the channel does not allow links and YouTube will auto-delete comments containing them; YouTube may delete comments with profanity or language it interprets as profanity; and sometimes comments disappear because of YouTube technical glitches. He says he generally does not personally delete ordinary comments, preferring bans only for serious rule violations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:56</a> | What was the ancient Near Eastern significance of festivals? | He says festivals usually centered on worship of gods, sometimes with harvest associations. | He says most ancient Near Eastern festivals were tied to ritual worship of deities. Some were connected to harvests or other events, such as a wheat harvest linked with a grain deity, but he says the majority were simply part of temple ritual calendars. Egyptian festival calendars, in his description, recorded the ongoing ritual life of the temple for posterity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8145" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:45</a> | Is Dagon's likeness a fish? | He says no; that is obsolete scholarship based on a Hebrew word association. | He explains that older scholarship connected Dagon with fish because Hebrew `dag` means fish, but he rejects that as an obsolete etymology. In his answer, Dagon is not a Semitic fish deity but a Philistine or Indo-European grain god. He also rejects attempts to connect Dagon with fish-suited Anunnaki imagery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:22</a> | Is baptism with fire final judgment, general winnowing, or both? | He says the chaff and unquenchable fire language points primarily to final judgment. | He reads Matthew 3:10-12 and focuses on the chaff being burned with unquenchable fire. He says that when chaff, tares, bad fish, or other refuse imagery is connected with unquenchable fire, it is almost always an allusion to final judgment. For that reason he rejects a general "winnowing of life" reading as the main sense of the passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:22</a> | Why did the Pharisees expect only the Messiah, Elijah, or the prophet to baptize if mikva'ot were common? | He says they objected to John performing the baptism, not to ritual bathing itself. | He explains that mikva'ot were common because people ritually bathed before going up to the temple, but they normally did that purification themselves. The Pharisees' question in John 1 is therefore not about ritual bathing existing, but about John officiating or performing a baptism over others. He says that kind of officiated baptism signaled the coming kingdom and would be expected from the Messiah, Elijah, or a prophet, which is why John's action was outside the normal mold. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8686" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:46</a> | Did people perform mikva'ot on their children at the time? | He says young children could be ritually bathed, but older children were expected to do it themselves. | He answers that it depended on the child's age. Children old enough to perform the ritual washing for themselves were expected to do so. Younger children could be washed, but he says that did not carry special ritual significance in the way the question seems to imply. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:17</a> | Where does baptism originate? | He traces it to ritual bathing, initially priestly and then broader, with symbolism shaped by temple-purification practice. | He traces baptism to ritual bathing, first performed by priests and later extended to everyone. He connects Jewish mikveh practice to temple purification, with background in Egyptian temple washing, and says baptism drew its symbolism from those bathing rites. He also says that in the Second Temple period baptism became an initiation symbol, especially through women converts entering the Jewish community, and John the Baptist made it more than ordinary temple-preparation washing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=8967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:27</a> | Which ziggurat was the Tower of Babel? | He says he thinks Babel was a ziggurat, but he does not know which one. | He declines to identify a specific ziggurat as the Tower of Babel. He says he thinks the tower was a ziggurat, but many ziggurats have not been excavated and he would need more detailed study before making any determination. His answer is deliberately limited to the general form, not the exact site. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=9091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:31</a> | Does it matter that early church fathers thought Pharaoh died? | He says no, because the fathers are valuable but not individually authoritative or always correct. | He says it does not matter much because the early church fathers, while valuable for theology and historical theology, did not get everything right. He rejects treating any individual father as authoritative on this point, and even notes that traditions appealing to the fathers often appeal to them as a plurality, where consensus is difficult because the writings are vast and varied. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=9247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:07</a> | How can academics know the meaning of words in languages no longer spoken? | He says lexicographers compare contexts, roots, cognate languages, and as many examples as possible. | He describes lexicography as studying a word across many texts and contexts to narrow its meaning. Scholars look at the sentence around the word, its root and related forms, its history, and cognate languages. He adds that unique words are especially difficult, and a follow-up confirms that even with a bilingual text scholars need at least some minimal understanding of the language, such as Coptic helped provide for Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=9683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:23</a> | Is 1 Chronicles 22:6-9 saying Jesus is God? | He says the passage is about Solomon and asks for clarification, not seeing Jesus directly in the cited text. | He checks 1 Chronicles 22:6-9 and says he is unsure how the question connects Jesus to the passage. He reads the text as David charging Solomon to build the temple, with verse 9 referring to Solomon as the son who will be a man of rest and receive peace in Israel. He does not see it as a direct reference to Jesus and says the question would need clarification for him to say more. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=9818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:38</a> | How can Christian laypeople publish scholarly papers? | He says they should find a suitable journal, follow its submission guidelines, and go through peer review like anyone else. | He says laypeople publish the same way others do: find a journal that publishes on the relevant subject, read that journal enough to know the article fits, follow the author or submission guidelines, and submit through peer review. He explains that reviewers may accept, reject, or require minor or major revisions, and warns that if requested revisions become absurdly extensive or agenda-driven, it may be better to reconsider that journal or turn the project into a book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=10155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:15</a> | What are his thoughts on Christianity in North Korea? | He says Christianity is extremely dangerous to practice there because allegiance to Christ threatens the regime's leader cult. | He describes North Korea as a communist state that has become a leader cult, making Christian allegiance especially dangerous. Practicing or smuggling Christianity can lead to labor camps, and he says missionary activity into North Korea is extremely risky. He mentions that Bibles are often smuggled on thumb drives through the Chinese border because media is tightly controlled and the Korean border is too militarized. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=10658" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:38</a> | How much money would need to be raised for an exodus excavation? | He says funding is only part of the issue; such a dig would also need institutional support and permissions. | He estimates that a decent dig might take roughly half a million dollars, while a fully funded dig could be closer to a million, but he immediately adds that money alone is not enough. A project would need permission, a worthwhile site, and site proofing; Avaris and Pi-Ramesses are already constrained by existing concessions or modern occupation. He says he personally would also need institutional backing because Egypt is stricter about excavation licenses and applications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=10857" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:57</a> | Could Associates for Biblical Research support an Egyptian dig? | He says ABR's model depends on third-party digs and volunteer fees, which does not fit Egypt's rules. | He says ABR normally works through third-party excavations by connecting volunteers and money to a dig that already has an affiliated archaeologist and institution. In his description, volunteers pay significant fees and those funds help both ABR and the dig. He says that model cannot operate in Egypt because charging diggers a fee is illegal there, so an Egyptian excavation has to secure money up front instead of funding itself through volunteer labor fees. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=10987" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:07</a> | Why has there not been major Christian institutional funding for biblical-maximalist excavation in Egypt? | He says many institutions focus on Israel, do not see Egypt's value, and face Egypt's restrictions on volunteer-funded digs. | He gives several reasons: many religious institutions that sponsor digs focus on Israel and do not see the value of Egypt even for Exodus studies; Egypt's ban on charging diggers is a major obstacle; and Egyptian rules expect projects to hire local workers rather than importing cheap outside labor. He also says many Old Testament scholars and seminaries lack deep Egyptological training, often favor Assyriology because of Semitic-language overlap, and therefore do not appreciate Egypt's relevance enough to fund major excavation work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=11786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:26</a> | If artificial intelligence reached consciousness, would it have a soul? | He says not necessarily, and treats the question as metaphysically open and hard to test. | He distinguishes weak AI, which only emulates consciousness, from strong AI, which would actually have a stream of consciousness. He says current large-language models are predictive systems rather than conscious minds. If a future complex AI did have consciousness, he says whether that amounted to a soul would remain an open question because testing for it would collapse or destroy the system one was trying to evaluate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=12446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:26</a> | How is forgiveness good if unrepentant people can harm again? | He distinguishes forgiving repentant people from releasing vengeance to God, and says forgiveness does not require giving someone a new chance to harm. | He distinguishes two kinds of forgiveness: forgiving a repentant person because God forgave first, and forgiving an unrepentant person by letting go and handing the matter to God. He says wisdom still matters, so forgiveness does not require giving a harmful person another opportunity to hurt you. He stresses that forgiveness is not a free pass, not necessarily forgetting, and not revenge; it leaves justice in God's hands and protects the injured person from living in bitterness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=13047" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:27</a> | Does the etymology of Samuel's name fit Saul better than Samuel? | He rejects that revision claim, noting Samuel and Saul have different tribal settings and the proposal does not fit the text. | He rejects the claim strongly because 1 Samuel gives Samuel's genealogy and identifies him with an Ephraimite line, while Saul is a Benjaminite from Gibeah. He says shared or similar names across tribes are not surprising in Israelite culture, and that making Samuel's naming story into Saul's story would require changing too much of Samuel's history. He also notes Elkanah is a theophoric name reused elsewhere, so the name evidence does not overturn the text's tribal and genealogical setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=13371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:51</a> | What is his assessment of Ay as the pharaoh of the exodus? | He rejects Ay because Ay's location and reign do not fit the required proximity to Goshen during the plagues. | He rejects Ay as the exodus pharaoh, first because of the "propinquity" problem: Moses, Israel's elders, taskmasters, and Pharaoh all need to be near enough for the plague narratives to unfold over months around Goshen, while Ay was based far away in Thebes. He also says Ay was an old priestly figure rather than a charioteer, making him a poor fit for the exodus pursuit. He does, however, think Ay may fit an oppression pharaoh who killed children, because Ay's actions helped trigger conflict with the Hittites and he had no moral obstacle to harsh policies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=13851" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:51</a> | Is there historical synchronization between Melchizedek and Abraham's time in Salem? | He connects Salem and Melchizedek to Jebusite deity names and says the Jebusite religious pattern differs from typical Canaanite pantheons. | He says Salem, or Uru-Salem, was connected with a Jebusite deity of peace, and Melchizedek's name reflects Zadok, which he identifies as a Jebusite god of righteousness. He describes Jebusite religion as distinct from typical Canaanite pantheons like those at Ugarit, because Jebusite deities seem to be named after ideals such as righteousness, peace, justice, and power. For Abraham's historical setting, he places the period around the 1720s BC, near Hammurabi and Neferhotep I, in a prosperous middle-bronze Canaan where Abraham could operate with flocks and armed retainers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=14543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:23</a> | Is Coptic considered a dead language, and why is it not treated as conversational today? | He says it is mostly liturgical and critically endangered, though a few families may still converse in it. | He says Coptic is usually classified as a liturgical language because it is mainly used in church services and religious texts, with sermons normally delivered in Arabic. He also says he has read of two families who still converse in Coptic, making it critically endangered rather than fully dead. He describes Bohairic Coptic as still speakable and closely related to late Egyptian, though very few people make the effort to converse in it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=14915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:35</a> | Are the Emerald Tablets of Thoth real Egyptian texts? | He says no; he calls them modern New Age fiction with no ancient Egyptian textual basis. | He says there is no Egyptian text called the Emerald Tablets of Thoth. He describes them as modern New Age fictional religious material, with no ancient Egyptian history, no ancient-source reference, and no basis as a real Egyptian text. He repeatedly frames the material as modern fantasy dressed up with Egyptian imagery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=15129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:12:09</a> | Does Jesus have one mind and will, or one according to each nature? | He says Christ has one mind belonging to one person, with both divine and human natures. | He rejects Nestorian-style separation of a divine person from a human person and also rejects Apollinarianism, where Christ's body is human but his mind is simply divine. In his answer, the incarnate Christ is one complete person with two natures, so his mind is both fully human and fully divine. He frames this as part of the historic Christian understanding that the whole second person of the Trinity is incarnate in Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=15336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:15:36</a> | Did all Egyptians ban pork? | He says almost all Egyptians avoided pork, with an exception for the cult of Set, where pigs could have ritual significance. | He says most Egyptians avoided pork, but the cult of Set was an exception because Set could be associated with a black pig, and Set worshippers could ritually raise and eat pigs. He explains the broader Egyptian aversion agriculturally: pigs polluted waterways, Egypt depended on canals for irrigation, and anything that fouled water was especially despised. This made pigs defiling and hated in most Egyptian settings, even if one cult used them ritually. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=15992" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:26:32</a> | How do people know what ancient languages sounded like if they have not been spoken for centuries? | He says it is partly guesswork, using living or later cognate languages, Coptic for Egyptian, and comparative phonology. | He says reconstructed pronunciation involves a lot of guesswork, but comparative evidence helps. Living or revived cognate languages can suggest likely sounds, and Coptic, as the final phase of Egyptian, helps with earlier Egyptian pronunciation. He cautions that missing vowels, dead languages like Akkadian, and consonantal shifts make the work imperfect, so scholars compare shared vocabulary, loanwords, graphemes, and phonetic developments to estimate how ancient languages roughly sounded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/bsrdrbRaZYQ?t=16298" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:31:38</a> | Why did Hinduism survive while Egyptian and Mesopotamian polytheism died out? | He says history differed by region: Mesopotamian polytheism gave way to Zoroastrianism and then Islam, while Egypt's was displaced by Christianity and later Islam. | He answers historically rather than theologically. Mesopotamian polytheism first gave way under Persian Zoroastrian influence and was later overtaken by Islam, while Egyptian polytheism was weakened by Christianity and then destroyed by Islam. India differed because Islam did not displace Hinduism as successfully; he says Hinduism was deeply tied to caste, agriculture, the Indus Valley setting, and practices such as the sacred role of cattle, so when Islamic rulers were removed many people reverted to Hinduism because it still fit their agricultural and social world. |
